title: Stovies
subtitle: Slow-cooked potato and leftover meat in beef dripping
excerpt: A Scottish dish of floury potatoes and leftover roast meat slow-cooked together in beef dripping or butter until the potatoes have almost dissolved into the rich pan juices. The original leftover-Sunday-roast supper.
sourceNotes: Stovies are one of the oldest documented Scottish dishes, traditionally made on Monday with the leftover meat and dripping from Sunday's roast. F. Marian McNeill, The Scots Kitchen (1929), documents stovies as a standard Scottish Monday dish. The name derives from the French étouffée (to smother or cook slowly), which entered Scots usage in the eighteenth century.

content.text: Stovies are the dish that makes good use of the dripping and leftover meat from a Sunday roast. The potatoes are cooked slowly in the fat with very little liquid; they absorb the meat juices and the flavour of the dripping and collapse into something dense, savoury, and deeply satisfying. The finished dish should be almost self-saucing: the potatoes at the bottom should be slightly sticky and caramelised, the ones on top soft and giving.

The meat is entirely flexible: leftover roast beef is traditional, but lamb, pork, or even sausages work well. The important thing is that the dripping comes from the same roast as the meat, or at least from a similar animal. Beef dripping and beef is the classic combination; lamb dripping with leftover lamb is equally good.

items.name: Potato
items.prepNote: floury variety, peeled and thickly sliced

items.name: Leftover roast beef
items.prepNote: or other roast meat, roughly torn or sliced

items.name: Beef stock
items.prepNote: or water, or leftover roasting juices

content.text: Melt {{beef-dripping}} of beef dripping or butter in a large, heavy-based saucepan over medium-low heat. Add {{onion}} thickly sliced onion and cook for 5 minutes until beginning to soften. Add a layer of {{potato}} thickly sliced potatoes, then a layer of {{leftover-roast-beef}} roughly torn or sliced leftover meat. Continue layering, seasoning generously between layers, finishing with a layer of potato on top. Tuck in the bay leaves.

Pour {{stock-beef}} of beef stock, water, or leftover roasting juices around the sides. The liquid should come only a quarter of the way up the potatoes. Cover tightly and cook over the lowest possible heat for 45 to 55 minutes, checking every 15 minutes to ensure nothing is sticking or burning. The potatoes at the bottom will soften and begin to fall apart; this is correct. Remove the bay leaves. Taste and adjust the seasoning.
Serve directly from the pot at the table. Oatcakes are the traditional accompaniment in Scotland.

items.fix: Use a heavy-based pan and the lowest possible heat. If burning is already happening, add a splash of water, reduce the heat, and continue.
items.cause: Heat was too high or the pot was not heavy enough
items.symptom: Potatoes have burned on the bottom

items.fix: Slice potatoes to 5 mm thickness, too thick and they take too long. Raise the heat slightly and continue cooking.
items.cause: Heat was too low, or the potatoes were cut too thick
items.symptom: Potatoes are still firm after 1 hour

items.fix: Season generously at each layer, stovies need salt throughout, not just at the end. Using leftover roasting juices or beef stock makes a significant difference.
items.cause: Water used instead of stock, or insufficient seasoning between layers

content.text: Stovies are as Scottish as any dish in the repertoire. A Monday meal made from the Sunday roast's leftovers, cooked in the dripping, and eaten from the pot. Early-twentieth-century Scottish cookery writers wrote them up as a standard of household cooking. The name comes from the French étouffée (to cook slowly in a covered pot with very little liquid). It came into Scots use through the long connection between Scotland and France. The dish varies by region and household. Some versions use corned beef, others sausage. The proportion of potato to meat shifts with what is to hand. What stays the same is the slow cooking in fat, the small amount of liquid, and the texture the potatoes get after an hour over low heat.
